summary: Stellar set with powerful Foolish Heart and Death Don't Have No Mercy
blurb: The show delivers scorching Garcia on Brown Eyed Women, a soaring Foolish Heart opener, and a brooding Death Don't Have No Mercy. Brent’s organ work drives the mid‑set while the Drum/Space segment peaks with haunting intensity. Highlights include The Weight, Looks Like Rain and a rousing encore of Black Muddy River.
review: April 2, 1990 at Atlanta’s Omni captured one of the spring ’90 high points. Jerry Garcia was on fire – his lead on Brown Eyed Women was described as “burned like hell,” and his all‑Jerry solo on Foolish Heart had fans saying it was a moment you’ll never hear again. Brent Mydland’s organ swelled through Death Don't Have No Mercy, giving the song a “phenomenal” vocal verse and a jazzy swing that lifted the whole second set. The Drum/Space segment was called a “king‑size” piece of 1990, haunting and powerful, leading into a tight run of The Other One and a soulful Close with Black Muddy River. Across soundboard, matrix and audience sources the band is consistently praised for tight interplay, especially Phil’s solid bass foundation and Bob Weir’s confident rhythm work. Whether you hear the crystal‑clear Dodd SBD or the killer matrix mix, the performance shines through – a must‑listen night of classic Dead magic.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Out of 11 recording analyses, 10 are strongly positive and only one is mixed. Reviewers repeatedly use terms like “stellar,” “scorching,” “magic,” and highlight the same standout songs (Foolish Heart, Looks Like Rain, The Weight, Death Don't Have No Mercy). This consistent enthusiasm warrants a high sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard capture – crisp, clean audio with no drop‑outs; showcases the full set’s dynamics and highlights (Foolish Heart, Looks Like Rain, The Weight) perfectly.

key_highlights: Jerry's scorching guitar on Brown Eyed Women and all‑Jerry Foolish Heart solo
Brent’s organ/vocal performance on Death Don't Have No Mercy
King‑size Drum/Space segment that builds into The Other One
Standout renditions of The Weight, Looks Like Rain, and a rousing Black Muddy River encore

band_performance.Jerry: Scorching lead work throughout – ‘burned like hell’ on Brown Eyed Women, unforgettable solo on Foolish Heart and magical phrasing on The Weight.
band_performance.Phil: Tight, prominent bass that drives the set; reviewers note a strong presence from the start and a bright mix that lets his lines shine.
band_performance.Bob: Confident rhythm guitar with vocal harmonies; praised as “Bobby is great” in the first set and adds solid support to the jam sections.
band_performance.Brent: Phenomenal organ work and vocal contributions, especially on Death Don't Have No Mercy; described as jazzy, swinging and even ‘shakin’ like Ray Charles.’
band_performance.Mickey: Haunting drum textures that fuel the Drum/Space segment, called a “king‑size” moment of 1990.
band_performance.Billy: Precise, powerful drumming throughout; high‑hats and overall groove noted as tight and driving the band’s energy.
